books.title: Everyone Else Seems to Sleep Fine
books.subtitle: For the wide-awake who feel they're falling behind — measuring a restless night against everyone who looks calm
books.desc: At 3am it feels like everyone else figured it out — sleeps fine, stays calm, knows some secret you're missing. This is a book about the comparison that runs loudest in the dark.
books.long_desc:
At 3am, it feels like you're the only one still awake.

Everyone else seems to have figured something out. They sleep through the night. They stay calm in the meetings that leave you wired for hours. They seem to know some secret about the market, the work, the way to live — a secret you keep almost grasping and never quite holding. And here you are, wide awake again, certain you're falling behind in a race you can't even see the track of.

This book is for the mind that measures itself against everyone else and always comes up short. The one trained to benchmark, to compare positions, to read the room for the edge you're missing — until the comparing turns inward and runs all night. You're not lazy and you're not failing. You've just pointed a high-performance instrument at the wrong target: yourself, in the dark, against people whose nights you will never actually see.

Night by night, you'll learn to recognize comparison as it rises — the reflexive ranking, the certainty that everyone else is fine — and to feel how little it has ever told you that was true. You'll learn that the calm you imagine in others is a story you're writing, and that the secret you think you're missing was never the thing keeping you awake.

Nothing here will help you win the comparison. That's the point. There is no position to optimize at 3am, no one to beat. There is only the slow recognition that the race was never real — and that you are allowed to stop running it long enough to rest.

The comparing mind may still speak. But you can stop letting it set the terms of the night.

Book 6 of the Still Hour Press series.
books.angle: They all seem to know a secret you're missing.
books.author: Dora Sable

books.title: The Body That Won't Stand Down
books.subtitle: For the wired and sleepless — when your nervous system keeps sounding an alarm at 3am that isn't there
books.desc: You lie down to rest and your body braces instead, sure something is wrong it can't name. This is a book about the alarm that keeps firing in the dark — and why it isn't a malfunction.
books.long_desc:
It's the middle of the night and your body is certain something is wrong.

Not your mind, exactly. Your mind knows the doors are locked, the work is handled, the morning is hours off. But your chest is tight, your jaw is set, and somewhere underneath, an old alarm is going off — the same one that runs the markets while you sleep, that wakes you at 3am to recalculate a decision you already made.

This book doesn't try to shut the alarm off. It does something quieter and more useful: it shows you what the alarm is for. You spent years building a life around eliminating variables, and now you're lying in the dark with the one variable you can't engineer away. The body sounding the warning isn't broken. It's doing exactly what it learned to do.

Night by night, you'll learn to recognize the false alarm as it rises — the bracing, the scanning, the chest and the phone — without needing it to stop before you can rest. Not through sleep rules or breathing drills sold as fixes, but through the slow work of recognition. The kind you can do at 3am, alone, with nothing to optimize.

You won't be cured. The alarm may still come. But you'll know what it is, and why it makes sense, and you'll no longer be alone with it in the dark.

Book 1 of the Still Hour Press series.
books.angle: Your body is bracing for danger that never comes.
books.author: Dora Sable

books.title: Who You Were Before the Grind
books.subtitle: For the sleepless who are quietly mourning — the person, the time, the life that the pace took
books.desc: Some of what keeps you awake isn't worry. It's grief — for the person you were before this, for the years the pace took. This is a book about the sorrow that surfaces in the dark.
books.long_desc:
Not all of what keeps you awake is anxiety. Some of it is grief.

It surfaces at night, when the work goes quiet and there's nothing left to optimize. A sense of something lost that you can't quite file. The person you were before you started thinking in probabilities. The friendships that thinned while you were heads-down. The years that went somewhere, traded for a payoff that was supposed to be worth it — and on the dark nights, you're not sure it was.

This book is for the one who has been too busy to mourn. Who lost time and people and a whole earlier self to the grind, and never stopped long enough to feel it, because grieving the trade felt like admitting the trade was a mistake. So the sorrow waited. And now it shows up at 3am, wearing the mask of restlessness, because the daylight never had room for it.

Night by night, you'll learn to recognize grief when it's pretending to be anxiety — the ache under the alertness, the loss under the worry. You'll learn that some of what your body holds at night isn't a problem to solve but a sorrow that has been waiting to be felt. Not fixed. Felt.

There's no closure to chase here, no stage to complete. There is only the quiet permission to let the grief be present — to stop treating your own mourning as one more inefficiency, and to let the dark hold what the day couldn't.

The loss is real. It deserved to be felt. And feeling it, at last, is how the night begins to soften.

Book 5 of the Still Hour Press series.
books.angle: Some of what keeps you up is grief.
books.author: Dora Sable

books.title: Too Many Tabs Open
books.subtitle: For minds that can't close at night — when you're exhausted and wide awake at the same time
books.desc: You lie down and the whole list arrives at once — every open loop, every unfinished thread, all of it loud and equally on fire. This is a book about the night the mind refuses to close.
books.long_desc:
You lie down, and instead of one quiet thought, the whole list arrives.

Every open loop. Every unanswered message. Every decision you stress-tested all day and a dozen you didn't get to. They come in together, all at once, all of them somehow equally urgent, none of them willing to wait until morning. You are exhausted and you are wide awake, and the gap between those two facts is where the night goes.

This book is for the mind that runs everything in parallel and can't find the button to close. The one trained to keep every variable in view, because the moment you stop watching is the moment something slips. You don't have an attention problem. You have a system that was built to never let anything fall — and now it won't let you fall asleep either.

Night by night, you'll learn to recognize overwhelm for what it is: not a character flaw, not a failure of discipline, but a nervous system holding too much because it was taught that holding it all was the job. You'll learn to feel the difference between the things that are actually moving and the ones that are simply loud. Not by emptying the list. By loosening your grip on it in the dark.

Nothing here asks you to get more organized. There is no system to adopt at 3am. There is only the quieter recognition that you are allowed to set it down — and that the world keeps turning while you do.

You won't finish everything. You were never going to. But you can stop carrying all of it into the night.

Book 2 of the Still Hour Press series.
books.angle: Everything feels urgent. Nothing will wait.
books.author: Dora Sable

books.title: The Sleep You Think You Haven't Earned
books.subtitle: For high performers who feel rest is irresponsible — and quietly believe they should be able to fix this by now
books.desc: You have everything you optimized for, and still you lie awake — sure that needing rest is a weakness, that you should have solved this by now. This is a book about the shame underneath the sleeplessness.
books.long_desc:
Somewhere along the way, rest started to feel like something you had to earn.

And you haven't earned it — not tonight, not by the measure you carry. There's always one more thing you could have handled, one more variable you should have caught. The markets run while you sleep, so sleep itself feels faintly irresponsible. And underneath that, quieter and sharper, is the part that's certain: someone like you, with everything you've optimized, should have this handled by now. The fact that you don't feels like a verdict.

This book is for the high performer who has turned a sleepless body into evidence of failure. Who minimizes the chest pain as stress, the wakefulness as weakness, and treats needing rest as proof that something is wrong with them rather than with the pace. The shame is so familiar you've stopped noticing it's there. You just know you should be better at this.

Night by night, you'll learn to recognize the shame for what it is — not the truth about you, but an old story about what you're allowed to need. You'll learn to hear the difference between the voice that drives you and the voice that simply judges you in the dark. Not by silencing it. By seeing it clearly enough that it loses its grip.

Nothing here will tell you to try harder, or to finally fix the thing you've been failing to fix. There is no last technique. There is only the recognition that you were never required to earn your own rest — and that the body asking for it is not betraying you.

The shame may still speak. But you can stop taking it as the final word.

Book 4 of the Still Hour Press series.
books.angle: Lying awake feels like proof you're failing.
books.author: Dora Sable

books.title: The 3am Replay
books.subtitle: For the mind that loops at night — running the same worry on a circuit that won't close
books.desc: One thought catches at 3am and then it loops — the same worry, the same replay, around and around until the sky goes grey. This is a book about the spiral, and how to step out of the circuit.
books.long_desc:
It starts with one thought. By 3am it's a circuit.

The same worry, played and replayed — a decision from yesterday, a sentence in an email, a number that won't sit right. Your mind treats it like a trade to stress-test, running the scenario forward and back, certain that if you just think it through one more time you'll find the move that makes the fear stop. So you go around again. And again. And the sky goes grey before the loop does.

This book is for the mind that mistakes spiraling for solving. The one trained to run worst-case scenarios on a loop, because somewhere you learned that vigilance is what keeps the floor from falling out. The spiral feels like problem-solving. It even feels responsible. But at 3am there is nothing to solve — only a groove the mind keeps falling into, deeper with every pass.

Night by night, you'll learn to recognize the spiral as it begins — the first catch, the pull toward the loop — and to feel the difference between thinking that moves and thinking that only circles. Not by forcing the thought away, which only feeds it, but by seeing the machinery of the loop clearly enough that you stop mistaking it for the truth.

There's no thought-stopping trick here, no script to repeat until the worry breaks. There is only the slow recognition that the loop is not the danger it imitates — and that you can let the circuit run without being carried around by it.

The thought may still catch. But you don't have to ride it until dawn.

Book 3 of the Still Hour Press series.
books.angle: The same thought, around and around, all night.
books.author: Dora Sable

books.title: The One Who Watches You Sleep
books.subtitle: For minds that monitor their own rest — when the part standing guard never learned how to step down
books.desc: There's a part of you that never goes off duty — that watches whether you're calm enough, asleep yet, doing it right. This is a book about the watcher, and how the monitoring became the problem.
books.long_desc:
There's a part of you that never clocks out. Even now. Even here.

It watches you try to fall asleep. It checks whether you're calm enough yet, whether the breathing is working, whether you're finally getting it right. It monitors the night the way you've monitored everything else — vigilant, never off duty, certain that the moment it stops watching is the moment something goes wrong. And so you lie there, being observed by yourself, and the watching is the very thing keeping you awake.

This book is for the mind that has turned self-surveillance into a way of life. The one that learned, somewhere, that safety meant never looking away — from the markets, the metrics, the self. After the false alarms and the spirals, the shame and the grief, this is the watcher underneath all of it: the part that has been standing guard at the gate of your own rest for so long it has forgotten how to step down.

Night by night, you'll learn to recognize the watcher — not as an enemy to defeat, but as something that has been working overtime to keep you safe, and is exhausted too. You'll learn that you don't have to manage your way into rest, or monitor whether the letting-go is going well. The watching can soften. The guard can sit down. And in the gap where the monitoring used to be, something quieter has room to arrive.

There is no final technique here, no last thing to get right. This is where the series has been going all along: not toward a fix, but toward the moment you stop standing over your own night — and simply let it hold you.

The watcher may still stir. But it no longer has to run the dark.

Book 7 of the Still Hour Press series.
books.angle: The part keeping watch is keeping you awake.
books.author: Dora Sable

books.title: The 3am Emergency That Isn't
books.subtitle: For the Wired Mind That Treats Every Late-Night Thought Like a Crisis
books.desc: The thought wakes you certain something is wrong, and your mind goes straight to work on it. This book is for the late-night brain that keeps sounding alarms over things that can wait until morning.
books.long_desc:
The thought arrives in the dark and it feels like an emergency.

Something you forgot. Something you said. A number that didn't add up, a message that landed wrong, a decision that suddenly looks like a mistake. Whatever it is, it has the weight of a crisis, and your mind — the one that's paid all day to catch problems early — snaps awake to handle it. By morning it will look small. At 3am it feels like the only thing in the world.

This book is for the sharp, responsible mind that mistakes its own alarm for actual danger. It doesn't tell you the thought is irrational or hand you a breathing trick to make it disappear. It shows you what a false alarm is — why a nervous system built for fast threat-detection keeps firing in a quiet bedroom where nothing is actually happening, and why the urgency you feel is real even when the emergency is not.

Night by night, you'll learn to recognize the signal for what it is. Not to argue with it. Not to prove it wrong. Just to notice the difference between a problem that needs you now and an alarm that only feels that way in the dark.

You'll still wake sometimes, certain. But you'll stop having to treat certainty as proof.

Book 1 of the Still Hour Press series.
books.angle: It feels urgent. At 3am, almost everything does.
books.author: Quinn Alder

books.title: One Thought Pulls the Next
books.subtitle: For the Analytical Mind That Can't Stop Solving at Night
books.desc: It starts with one thought, and your mind reaches for the next, and the next, until the thinking is no longer about anything. This book is for the brain that spirals because solving is the only thing it knows how to do.
books.long_desc:
It never starts as a spiral. It starts as a good idea.

One thought to work out, and your mind — quick, capable, the part of you that gets paid to think things all the way through — takes hold of it. Then it reaches for the next thread, and the next. Tomorrow's meeting. Last quarter's call. The thing you should have said. Somewhere in the descent the thinking stops being about any of it and becomes its own current, pulling faster the harder you try to climb out.

This book is for the analytical mind that turns its greatest strength against itself at night. It doesn't tell you to stop thinking or to empty your head — advice that has never once worked on a mind like yours. It shows you how the spiral actually moves: why a brain built to solve treats every thought as a problem, why effort feeds the loop instead of ending it, and what happens when you stop supplying the next thread.

Night by night, you'll learn to feel the pull the moment it begins and step off the current — not by winning the argument in your head, but by seeing there was never an argument to win. The thinking can keep going without you. You don't have to follow it down.

You'll still catch yourself circling sometimes. But you'll know how to stop feeding it.

Book 2 of the Still Hour Press series.
books.angle: You're not thinking it through. You're going in circles.
books.author: Quinn Alder

books.title: The Part of You That Never Clocks Out
books.subtitle: For the Mind That Keeps Watch Over Itself Long After Dark
books.desc: There's a part of you that monitors everything — your work, your words, even your own thoughts — and it doesn't switch off at night. This book is for the mind that can't stop watching itself.
books.long_desc:
There's a part of you that never goes off duty.

It tracks how the meeting landed and how you came across in it. It reviews the day for anything you missed and scans tomorrow for anything that could go wrong. Late at night, when there's nothing left to manage, it turns inward and starts watching the one thing still moving — your own mind. Are you overthinking again? Why aren't you asleep yet? The watching becomes the thing that keeps you awake.

This book is for the vigilant, high-performing mind that has been standing guard so long it forgot it was allowed to stop. It doesn't tell you to silence the watcher or fight the part of you that monitors everything — that part has kept you sharp, and it isn't the enemy. It shows you where the watcher came from, why a mind under years of pressure learns to never look away, and what it feels like to let the guard sit down without anything falling apart.

Night by night, you'll learn to notice the watching itself — to recognize the observer behind the thoughts and let it soften, not by trying harder to relax, but by seeing that nothing actually requires your supervision in the dark.

The watcher won't disappear. But it can finally rest, and so can you.

Book 3 of the Still Hour Press series.
books.angle: Someone in you is still on duty. It's you.
books.author: Quinn Alder

books.title: The 3am Alarm That Isn't Real
books.subtitle: For High-Pressure Minds Whose Bodies Won't Stand Down at Night
books.desc: Wired and exhausted at 3am, your body is bracing for a threat that isn't in the room. This book names the false alarm so you can stop fighting it.
books.long_desc:
You're not tired. You're wired and tired, which is worse.

The day is over. The laptop is shut, the threads have gone quiet, the markets are closed. And still, the moment your head hits the pillow, something in your chest tightens like a deal is about to go sideways. Heart a little fast. Mind scanning. Ready. For nothing.

This book is for the engineer, the trader, the operator who lies in the dark wondering why the body won't stand down when there is nothing left to do. It doesn't promise to switch the feeling off. It names what's happening — a false alarm, an old survival system firing on a quiet night — in language you can reach for the next time it wakes you.

Night by night, you'll come to understand the alarm instead of bracing against it. Not through harder discipline or one more wind-down protocol, but through recognition: seeing the system for what it is, letting the charge move through, and finding the floor underneath it.

You'll still have nights like this. But you'll know what your body is doing, and why it makes sense, and you won't be so alone with it at 3am.

Book 1 of the Still Hour Press sleep series.
books.angle: Your body is sounding an alarm. Nothing's wrong.
books.author: Suki Bennett

books.title: Too Many Tabs to Sleep
books.subtitle: For High-Load Minds That Can't Find the Off Switch at Night
books.desc: The day loaded too much in and never closed it out, so now your mind can't power down. This book is for the overloaded brain that doesn't know how to stop.
books.long_desc:
You got into bed with forty tabs still open. None of them on a screen.

The unanswered message. The number that didn't land. The decision you're carrying into tomorrow. All day the input kept coming and there was never a clean moment to set it down, so it followed you here, into the one place that was supposed to be quiet. And now the system that ran all day at full load simply will not idle.

This book is for the high-performer whose mind feels like a browser with too much running — the founder, the analyst, the operator who lies awake not spiraling, exactly, just unable to find the off switch. It doesn't hand you a shutdown routine to execute perfectly. It names the overload for what it is and shows you how a flooded nervous system actually empties.

Night by night, you'll learn the difference between forcing your mind to stop and letting the load come down on its own. Not more control. Less. A way of setting things down that doesn't require finishing them first.

You'll still have full days. But the night will stop being one more thing the day spills into.

Book 2 of the Still Hour Press sleep series.
books.author: Suki Bennett
keywords: can't sleep too much on my mind

books.title: The Loop That Keeps You Up
books.subtitle: For Anyone Whose 3am Thoughts Feed on Themselves
books.desc: It starts with one thought at 3am, and then it loops, and each lap makes the next one worse. This book is for the mind that gets caught in the spiral and can't climb out.
books.long_desc:
It always starts small. One thought. Then it comes back, a little louder.

By the third lap you're rehearsing tomorrow's meeting, then last quarter's mistake, then the conversation you should have handled differently, and somewhere in there the thinking stops being about anything and becomes its own engine — spinning faster the more you try to think your way out. You're not solving. You know you're not solving. You can't stop.

This book is for the sharp, analytical mind that turns on itself at 3am — the kind of person whose greatest strength all day becomes the thing that traps them at night. It doesn't tell you to clear your mind or count your breaths until the spiral magically lifts. It names how the loop actually works, why your problem-solving brain feeds it, and how a spiral loses its fuel.

Night by night, you'll learn to recognize the loop the moment it starts and step off it — not by winning the argument in your head, but by seeing that there was never an argument to win. The thinking can keep going. You don't have to ride it.

You'll still wake at 3am sometimes. But the loop won't own the rest of the night.

Book 3 of the Still Hour Press sleep series.
books.angle: One thought. Then it starts circling.
books.author: Suki Bennett
